Healthcare Information Systems · CMU 67250

The equipment exists.
It just can't get to
where it's needed.

MedShare Network is a real-time cloud platform that transforms idle hospital equipment into a shared, intelligent resource — connecting hospitals, reducing waste, and closing equity gaps in healthcare.

Explore the Solution → See the Problem Live App Demo

scroll
01 — The Problem

A system built for abundance,
not for equity

Medical equipment is underutilized, unevenly distributed, and never reused effectively across healthcare networks.

Hospitals operate in a deeply unequal system. A well-funded academic medical center may have equipment sitting unused while a community hospital in a low-income neighborhood cannot afford the same device.

There is no shared visibility, no coordination, and no incentive to share. Equipment accumulates where resources are abundant and never reaches the people who need it most.

The result: critical devices like ventilators and infusion pumps sit idle in storage rooms while patients in under-resourced hospitals go without. This is not a supply problem — it's an information and coordination failure.

🏥
Large AMCs
Equipment holders with excess inventory
🏘️
Community Hospitals
Under-resourced, high unmet need
👩‍⚕️
Clinical Staff
Nurses, logistics, administrators
42%
of all mobile hospital devices sit idle at any given time, representing enormous untapped capacity1
$1.9T
wasted on unnecessary medical care in the US each year — much driven by redundant equipment procurement2
152
rural hospitals have closed or cut critical services since 2010, deepening geographic health inequity3
0
existing platforms provide real-time cross-hospital equipment matching at network scale. The gap is real.
02 — The Solution

MedShare Network: equipment hoarding
becomes equipment sharing

A real-time cloud-based platform using RFID, AI demand prediction, and automated transfer workflows to match idle equipment to hospitals that need it — before shortages happen.

STEP 01
LOG
RFID tags log every device's location & status in real time
STEP 02
SEARCH
AI searches the full network for available matching devices
STEP 03
MATCH
Optimal transfer path calculated with urgency weighting
STEP 04
TRANSFER
Both hospitals notified, reviewed, and confirm transfer
STEP 05
TRACK
Full chain of custody documented throughout delivery
1
RFID Real-Time TrackingLocation & status of every device across the network, streamed at 2.4M reads/day
2
AI Demand PredictionML forecasts shortages up to 6 hours ahead with 94% confidence scores
3
Compliance DocumentationAutomated sterilization records and chain-of-custody logs for every transfer
4
Department AlertsCritical, high, and medium priority notifications per department in real time
5
Role-Based AccessClinical, logistics, and admin views tailored to each user type
6
EHR IntegrationConnects to existing hospital inventory and electronic health record systems
⚡

Interactive Application Prototype

Explore the fully interactive MedShare app — built with Figma AI from our team's wireframes. Click through all screens and flows.

Open Live Demo

Data Sources Dashboard

The central hub aggregating all real-time data streams into MedShare. RFID tags stream 2.4M readings per day, giving the network a live heartbeat of every device across all member hospitals.

Four integrated data sources — RFID Tags, Checkout Logs, Equipment DB, and Maintenance API — all show live health status and sync frequency, giving administrators immediate visibility into any degradation.

RFID Tags — 847 devices2.4M reads/day23 checkout logsEquipment DB realtimeMaintenance API 120/day+18% activity trend

Real-Time Location Tracking

RFID-powered live equipment feed shows every device's exact location, status, and battery level across the network. Staff can immediately see whether a device is in-use, available, or in transit between hospitals.

847 active tags with 96% average signal strength provide the visibility backbone that makes the entire platform possible. Live updates show room-level precision for every tracked device.

847 Active Tags12 In Transit96% Signal AvgIN-USE statusAVAILABLE statusTRANSIT status

Department Alerts

Real-time notifications surface critical, high, and medium-priority equipment shortages directly to department staff. Emergency (3 alerts), ICU (5 alerts), Surgery (2), and Cardiology (1) each receive their own tailored alert feed.

Critical alerts like low infusion pump stock or an urgent ventilator need surface immediately. Staff acknowledge with a single tap, creating an audit trail of response times across the network.

CRITICAL priorityHIGH priorityMEDIUM priorityPer-department viewsLow Stock alertsOne-tap acknowledge

AI Demand Prediction

ML-powered forecasting analyzes historical usage patterns, seasonal trends, and patient admission data to predict equipment shortages up to 6 hours before they occur — with 94% confidence scores.

The 7-day demand trend chart allows procurement and logistics teams to proactively arrange transfers. Prediction alerts flag High Demand for infusion pumps at Community General and Low Supply of ventilators at Metro Health.

7-Day Demand Trend94% confidenceUpcoming predictionsHIGH urgencyMEDIUM urgencyPer-hospital alerts

Maintenance Scheduling

Equipment service scheduling and tracking ensures transferred devices are always safe and regulatory-compliant. 12 services due this week, 3 overdue, 47 completed — with an average service time of 2.3 hours.

Overdue maintenance alerts prevent unsafe equipment from entering the transfer pool. The "Schedule Maintenance" CTA surfaces immediately when action is required, keeping all member hospitals accountable.

12 due this week3 overdue47 completed2.3h avg timeCalibration & InspectionAction Required alerts

Transfer Workflow

The end-to-end transfer workflow guides hospitals through Equipment Request → System Check → Routing → Confirmation. Every step is logged, creating a complete chain of custody for compliance and accountability.

Active requests show the full context: Infusion Pump (HIGH priority), requested by Community General Ward 3B for Patient #4829, matched from Metro Health Center — all visible in real time.

4-step workflowAI-powered routingIN PROGRESS trackingBi-directional confirmPatient-linked requestsFull audit trail
03 — Strategic Analysis

Creating value across the entire
healthcare value chain

MedShare operates as a nonprofit platform optimizing for access over profit — a strategic positioning that unlocks network effects unavailable to for-profit competitors.

📦
Procurement
Hospitals access shared equipment instead of purchasing new devices outright
⚙️
Operations
Idle equipment matched in real time, reducing carrying and storage costs
🏥
Clinical Care
Ventilators and infusion pumps reach patients faster across the network
🤝
Community
Smaller hospitals gain access to devices they could not otherwise afford
🌐
Network Scale
Every new hospital makes the entire platform more valuable for all members
Competitive advantage: Nonprofit status enables trust-based participation that a for-profit model cannot replicate — hospitals share data they would never expose to a commercial competitor
Scalability: Membership model funds operations; network effects compound value with every hospital that joins the platform
Defensible moat: AI demand prediction improves as more hospitals contribute data — the algorithm gets smarter for everyone with every new member
Sustainability: Tiered membership fees + grant funding + demonstrated cost savings creates a stable long-term financial model
🔗
Business Model
Nonprofit SaaS. Hospitals pay annual membership fees tiered by bed count. Grant funding supplements core operations. No per-transfer fees to maximize participation incentives across economic levels.
📊
Competitive Positioning
No direct competitors operate at regional network scale with AI-driven demand forecasting. Existing solutions are either single-hospital inventory tools or manual brokerage services. MedShare's network data becomes a durable moat.
🚀
Adoption Strategy
Pilot with 3–5 regional hospitals. Demonstrate measurable reduction in idle equipment and procurement costs within 6 months. Use documented ROI and equity outcomes to drive regional expansion through health system partnerships.
04 — Social Theory Application

Understanding why technology
succeeds or fails

Three social theories ground our understanding of how MedShare will be adopted, adapted, and challenged within complex hospital environments.

01
🖥️
Technology Acceptance Model
TAM — Davis (1989) · Venkatesh et al. (2003)
Staff must perceive MedShare as both useful and easy to use. In healthcare environments where cognitive load is already high, any added friction will cause adoption to stall — regardless of the platform's technical merits.
"A simple, intuitive interface matters as much as the underlying technology. If clinicians perceive MedShare as a burden, they will work around it."
02
⚙️
Sociotechnical Systems Theory
Trist & Bamforth (1951) · Human Relations
MedShare operates inside complex social systems — hospital hierarchies, procurement politics, inter-departmental competition. Successful implementation requires change management and governance that gives all member hospitals a meaningful voice in platform decisions.
"Technology and people must co-evolve. Governance that excludes smaller hospitals will reproduce the same power imbalances MedShare aims to fix."
03
⚖️
Privacy Calculus Theory
Culnan & Armstrong (1999) · Organization Science
Hospitals weigh benefits against perceived risks — exposing inventory data to competitors, liability for transferred equipment, loss of procurement leverage. Transparent data policies and strong governance tip the calculus toward participation.
"Hospitals will share when trust exceeds risk. Data governance, liability frameworks, and demonstrated confidentiality are not optional — they are the product."
05 — Expected Impact

Organizational efficiency
meets systemic equity

MedShare creates measurable value for individual hospitals while addressing systemic inequities in healthcare resource distribution.

🏢 Organizational Impact
  • Significant reduction in capital expenditure on duplicate medical equipment purchases across member hospitals
  • Decreased equipment idle time — from 42% to a projected under 20% within participating networks
  • Faster clinical response when critical equipment is needed, reducing patient wait times
  • Automated compliance documentation eliminates manual record-keeping burden for biomedical staff
  • Predictive alerts shift procurement from reactive purchasing to proactive resource management
  • EHR integration reduces duplicate data entry and improves data accuracy across systems
🌍 Societal Impact
  • Underserved community hospitals gain access to equipment previously out of financial reach
  • Geographic health inequities reduced as rural and low-income area hospitals join the network
  • Reduced healthcare waste contributes to environmental sustainability goals across the sector
  • Demonstrated model for cooperative healthcare infrastructure that can scale nationally
  • Network effects ensure the platform becomes more equitable as adoption grows — not less
  • Potential to prevent rural hospital closures by reducing capital equipment barriers
Risks & Unintended Consequences
⚠ Risk
Data Privacy Breach
Exposing hospital inventory data could reveal competitive vulnerabilities. Requires enterprise-grade encryption and strict access controls.
⚠ Risk
Equity Reinforcement
Without intentional design, platforms can reinforce existing inequalities. Membership fee structures must not exclude the hospitals most in need.
⚠ Risk
Liability in Transfer
Equipment that fails after transfer creates complex liability questions. Legal frameworks and insurance models must be established before launch.
⚠ Risk
Adoption Resistance
Procurement departments may resist a system that reduces their purchasing authority. ROI demonstration and change management are critical.
⚠ Risk
Over-Reliance on AI
Wrong predictions could leave hospitals without critical equipment. Human override capabilities and prediction transparency are essential safeguards.
⚠ Risk
RFID Infrastructure Gap
Smaller hospitals may lack RFID infrastructure. Phased rollouts with hardware support grants can address this barrier equitably.
👁️
Visibility is infrastructure.
Real-time information, without adding a single new device, can close critical gaps in care.
📈
Value grows with adoption.
Every new member hospital makes the network more useful for every other hospital.
🤝
Technology alone is not enough.
Cultural change and trust-building need to happen alongside the technical rollout.
⚖️
Equity from the start.
Without intentional design, platforms can reinforce the very inequalities they aim to fix.
06 — Team Collaboration

Team SRS — Carnegie Mellon University

67250 Information Systems Milieux · Spring 2026

SG
Siri Ganapathineedi
Strategic Analysis Lead
Led value chain analysis and competitive positioning research. Developed the nonprofit business model framework and sustainability strategy. Coordinated stakeholder analysis and equity impact assessment.
SP
Serena Ploeger
Social Theory & Research Lead
Applied TAM, Sociotechnical Systems Theory, and Privacy Calculus Theory to the MedShare context. Conducted literature review and synthesized academic sources. Wrote the social insights and unintended consequences analysis.
RS
Riya Shenvi
Solution Design & UX Lead
Designed the platform concept, feature set, and system workflow. Created low-fidelity wireframes and coordinated the Figma AI mockups. Led the solution feasibility analysis and adoption challenge assessment.
📅 Collaboration Process & Timeline
Weeks 1–2
Problem Identification & Team Charter
Established team norms, roles, and decision-making processes. Brainstormed problem domains across healthcare, education, and civic tech. Selected medical equipment distribution after identifying the 42% idle device statistic.
Weeks 3–4
Research & Solution Conceptualization
Conducted background research using GE Healthcare reports, AJPH, and BMC Health Services Research. Developed the core MedShare concept. Debated RFID vs. manual tracking, deciding on RFID + AI as the differentiating features.
Weeks 5–6
Strategic Analysis & Theory Application
Mapped solution to value chain framework. Selected TAM, Sociotechnical Systems Theory, and Privacy Calculus as the most relevant social theories. Integrated equity considerations throughout all three theory applications.
Weeks 7–8
Wireframing, Mockups & Website
Created low-fidelity wireframes for all six platform screens. Used Figma AI to develop high-fidelity interactive mockups. Built the project website. Designed and iterated the 24×36 poster through three drafts before finalizing.
Week 9
Final Review & Poster Session
Conducted peer review of all deliverables. Refined messaging and visual consistency. Submitted poster for printing by the April 24 deadline. Presented at the April 27 Simmons Auditorium poster session.
Project Poster
REFERENCES
1 Horblyuk et al. / GE Healthcare — Medical device utilization rates in acute care settings.
2 Speer et al. (2020). American Journal of Public Health (AJPH) — Estimates of waste in US healthcare spending.
3 Balakrishnan et al. (2026). BMC Health Services Research — Rural hospital closures and service reductions since 2010.
4 Davis, F.D. (1989). Perceived usefulness, perceived ease of use, and user acceptance of information technology. MIS Quarterly.
5 Venkatesh et al. (2003). User acceptance of information technology. MIS Quarterly.
6 Trist & Bamforth (1951). Some social and psychological consequences of the longwall method of coal-getting. Human Relations.
7 Culnan & Armstrong (1999). Information privacy concerns, procedural fairness, and impersonal trust. Organization Science.
